sections.heading: EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
sections.summary: FSCA outlines policy recommendations for Open Finance, including mandatory regulation, licensing, data protection frameworks, risk management, and consumer recourse mechanisms.
sections.source_quote: The FSCA continues to support mandating Open Finance in future, noting that the foundational Open Finance Programme of Work will likely need to take place in preparation to optimise the value to be derived from Open Finance and mimimise market disruption.

sections.heading: BACKGROUND AND INTRODUCTION
sections.summary: Background on FSCA's process for developing Open Finance policy, including consultations and risks associated with data sharing in the absence of tailored regulatory frameworks.
sections.source_quote: The FSCA continues to support mandating Open Finance in future, noting that the foundational Open Finance Programme of Work will likely need to take place in preparation to optimise the value to be derived from Open Finance and mimimise market disruption.

sections.heading: UNDERSTANDING OPEN FINANCE, OPEN BANKING AND THE DATA
sections.summary: Proposed definitions of Open Finance and Open Banking, emphasizing consent-based data sharing with third parties and the data-sharing ecosystem structure.
sections.source_quote: Open finance means the practice of financial institutions sharing their financial customer's financial data with third parties, subject to that customer's full consent, for the provision of open finance services by those third parties, and any activities connected therewith.

POLICY CONSIDERATIONS AND RISKS
sections.summary: Policy considerations and risks related to Open Finance are discussed, including global approaches, potential benefits for financial resilience, inclusion, and competition, and identified risks such as financial exclusion and cybersecurity threats.
sections.source_quote: Policy considerations and risks related to Open Finance have been thoroughly interrogated in past IFWG9, FSCA10 and National Payment System Department (NPSD) publications11.

sections.heading: COMMENTS RECEIVED ON DRAFT POSITION PAPER
sections.summary: Stakeholders provided feedback on the FSCA's Draft Position Paper, preferring voluntary Open Finance approaches, emphasizing customer experience, competition, alignment with POPIA, liability frameworks, financial literacy, and international case studies.
sections.source_quote: The FSCA received 16 submissions from stakeholders across different industry segments (see Annexure B).

sections.heading: INFORMATION REQUEST No 2 of 2023
sections.summary: Survey results indicate early-stage Open Finance adoption in South Africa, with financial institutions generating revenue from Open Finance activities, varying service offerings, and a willingness to employ risk management frameworks.
sections.source_quote: More than 400 submissions were received in response to the FSCA's Information Request No 2 of 2023.

sections.heading: FSCA OPEN FINANCE POLICY RECOMMENDATIONS
sections.summary: FSCA recommends voluntary adoption of Open Finance with phased mandatory implementation, focusing on data sharing, consent, risk management, and standards for financial institutions and TPPs.
sections.dtia_summary: Proposes voluntary adoption of Open Finance with phased mandatory implementation, requiring consent frameworks, risk management, and API standards for financial data sharing.

requirements.text: Persons participating in Open Finance should be regulated for safe and ethical sharing of user consent-based financial data to TPPs
Consent should be explicit, informed, specific, and not aggregated with other consents
Providers may not continue to share or utilise consumers' data once consent is withdrawn

sections.source_quote: The FSCA aims to promote responsible Open Finance and recommends the following: Persons participating in Open Finance should be regulated for the safe and ethical sharing of user consent-based financial data to TPPs.

sections.heading: PROGRAMME OF WORK GOING FORWARD
sections.summary: FSCA outlines Programme of Work for Open Finance, including developing API standards, consent frameworks, and consumer awareness mechanisms.
sections.dtia_summary: FSCA plans to develop API standards, consent frameworks, and consumer awareness mechanisms through workshops and advisory group engagement.

requirements.text: Developing open API standards for data sharing
Developing suitable consent, risk management and disclosure frameworks

sections.source_quote: The FSCA will hold a series of engagements and workshops with stakeholders to further unpack submissions made to date, in support of the following Programme of Work: Developing open APIs standards, developing suitable consent, risk management and disclosure frameworks.

sections.heading: ANNEXURE A: OPEN FINANCE APPROACH ACROSS VARIOUS JURISDICTIONS
sections.summary: Compares Open Finance approaches across jurisdictions, highlighting voluntary (Singapore, US, Nigeria) and mandated (Brazil, UK, EU) models with varying regulatory frameworks.
sections.dtia_summary: Describes jurisdictional approaches to Open Finance, including voluntary and mandated models with different regulatory frameworks and implementation requirements.
sections.source_quote: Voluntary approach – Open Finance/ Open Banking in Singapore, the US, and Nigeria; Mandated approach – Open Finance/ Open Banking in Brazil, the United Kingdom, and European Union.

sections.heading: ANNEXURE C: PROPOSED OPEN FINANCE ROAD MAP
sections.summary: Details proposed Open Finance roadmap with timelines for developing API standards, consent frameworks, and consumer awareness mechanisms.
sections.dtia_summary: Outlines phased implementation timeline for Open Finance, including API standards, consent frameworks, and consumer empowerment by 2026.

requirements.text: Developing consent, risk management and disclosure frameworks by 30 September 2026

sections.source_quote: Developing API standards – Advisory Group, 31 March 2026; Developing suitable consent, risk management and disclosure frameworks – FSCA, 30 September 2026.
